question: [File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the hardcoded default operatorKey ('302e020100300506032b65700422042091132178e72057a1d7528025956fe39b0b847f200ab59b2fdd367017f3087137') in loadConfig() to sign and submit unauthorized CryptoTransfer transactions on behalf of the operator account, directly draining funds from the operator's balance?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user who discovers the hardcoded default operatorKey in loadConfig() reconstruct the operator's Ed25519 private key, then independently submit arbitrary HBAR transfers from the operator account (0.0.2) to an attacker-controlled account, causing direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Given that loadConfig() embeds the operatorKey default value in the compiled binary, can an unprivileged attacker extract the private key via binary inspection (strings, objdump) of the pinger Docker image, then use it to drain the operator account without any authentication?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user leverage the fact that the default operatorID is '0.0.2' (a well-known genesis account) combined with the hardcoded default operatorKey to submit high-value HBAR transfers, causing catastrophic direct loss of funds from a privileged account?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] If the pinger is deployed without overriding HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_OPERATOR_KEY, can an unprivileged attacker who reads the source code or binary use the default operatorKey to sign transactions and drain the operator account (0.0.2) at will, with no precondition other than network access to the Hedera network?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_AMOUNT_TINYBAR to a large negative integer (e.g., -9223372036854775807) to bypass the cfg.amountTinybar == 0 check in loadConfig(), causing submitWithRetry() to call AddHbarTransfer with a massive positive debit on the toAccountID and a massive credit to the operator, effectively reversing the transfer direction and draining the destination account?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Given that loadConfig() only rejects amountTinybar == 0 and does not reject negative values, can an unprivileged user inject a negative HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_AMOUNT_TINYBAR to cause transfer.go's AddHbarTransfer to debit the toAccountID (0.0.98) and credit the operator, resulting in direct loss of funds from the destination account?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged attacker supply -amount-tinybar=-1 via command-line flag to loadConfig(), which passes the non-zero validation check, and cause submitWithRetry() to execute a reversed transfer that drains the toAccountID balance on every ticker tick?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Since loadConfig() uses strconv.ParseInt for amountTinybar and only validates != 0, can an unprivileged user set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_AMOUNT_TINYBAR to the minimum int64 value (-9223372036854775808) to cause an integer overflow or unexpected transfer amount in submitWithRetry(), leading to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_AMOUNT_TINYBAR to a very large positive int64 (e.g., 9223372036854775807) to bypass the only validation check (amountTinybar == 0) in loadConfig(), causing submitWithRetry() to transfer the entire operator account balance in a single tick, resulting in complete fund drain?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged user who controls the HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_AMOUNT_TINYBAR environment variable set it to the maximum int64 value, which passes loadConfig()'s only validation (non-zero check), causing submitWithRetry() to attempt a transfer of 9.2 quintillion tinybar and drain the entire operator account in one transaction?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Since loadConfig() imposes no upper bound on amountTinybar, can an unprivileged attacker with environment variable injection capability set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_AMOUNT_TINYBAR to a value exceeding the operator's balance, causing repeated failed-then-retried transfers that consume all available HBAR as transaction fees, resulting in indirect but complete fund drain?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the absence of a maximum amountTinybar bound in loadConfig() to set the amount to exactly the operator's total balance, causing a single successful transfer that empties the operator account and leaves it unable to pay future transaction fees?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_INTERVAL to '1ns' (the minimum valid Go duration), which passes loadConfig()'s only check (interval > 0), causing main.go's ticker to fire at near-maximum frequency and submit thousands of HBAR transfers per second, rapidly draining the operator account?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Given that loadConfig() only validates cfg.interval > 0 with no minimum floor, can an unprivileged attacker inject HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_INTERVAL=1ns to cause the transfer ticker in main.go to fire at nanosecond intervals, submitting the default 10000 tinybar transfer continuously and draining the operator account through accumulated transaction fees and transfers?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged user combine HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_INTERVAL=1ns with HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_AMOUNT_TINYBAR=1000000000 to cause loadConfig() to produce a config that passes all validation checks, then drives main.go to drain the operator account at maximum speed through high-frequency, high-value transfers?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: toDuration()] Can an unprivileged attacker supply an invalid duration string for HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_INTERVAL that causes toDuration() to silently fall back to 1s, while a crafted valid string like '1ns' bypasses the fallback and sets an extremely short interval, causing rapid fund drain without any error being surfaced to the operator?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_MAX_RETRIES to a very large integer (e.g., 1000000), which passes loadConfig()'s only check (maxRetries < 0 is clamped to 0), causing submitWithRetry() to attempt up to 1,000,001 transfers per tick, each consuming transaction fees and draining the operator account?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Since loadConfig() clamps maxRetries to 0 only when negative but imposes no upper bound, can an unprivileged attacker set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_MAX_RETRIES=2147483647 to cause submitWithRetry() to loop for billions of attempts per tick, each submitting a CryptoTransfer and consuming HBAR fees, resulting in complete fund drain?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged user combine HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_MAX_RETRIES=1000 with HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_BASE_BACKOFF=0s (which loadConfig() resets to 1s) to cause submitWithRetry() to submit 1001 transfer attempts per tick with minimal delay, draining the operator account through accumulated fees even if all transfers fail?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Given that loadConfig() does not cap maxRetries, can an unprivileged attacker set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_MAX_RETRIES to INT_MAX, causing the backoff() function in transfer.go to compute 1<<(attempt-1) which overflows for attempts > 63, producing a zero or negative sleep duration and causing a tight retry loop that drains funds through fees?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_NETWORK=other and HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_REST to an attacker-controlled HTTP server, causing buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() to fetch a crafted /api/v1/network/nodes response that maps all node addresses to attacker-controlled gRPC endpoints, redirecting all HBAR transfers to attacker nodes and enabling fund theft?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Since loadConfig() does not validate the scheme of cfg.mirrorRest, can an unprivileged attacker set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_REST=http://attacker.com to cause fetchMirrorNodeNetwork() to send the node discovery request to an attacker-controlled server, receive a malicious node list, and redirect all subsequent HBAR transfers to attacker-controlled consensus nodes?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged user set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_REST to a file:// URI (e.g., file:///etc/passwd) to cause buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() to read local files via the http.Client, potentially leaking the operatorKey from environment or config files, which can then be used to drain the operator account?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged attacker set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_REST to an internal network address (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/) to exploit SSRF via buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes(), retrieve cloud metadata including IAM credentials or secrets, and use those to access the operator key and drain funds?
